speaker: Erica
en: Hey Ian, how far along are you on preparing the presentation on volcanoes next week?

speaker: Ian
en: Not much.

speaker: Erica
en: Maybe we can straighten things out first?

speaker: Ian
en: Sure.

speaker: Ian
en: I decided to start with shield volcano.

speaker: Ian
en: The name derives from its similarity in shape to a warrior's shield lying face up.

speaker: Ian
en: The Hawaiian shield volcanoes are the most famous examples.

speaker: Ian
en: Professor Joan mentioned that many scholars have already done quite a lot of research in this field.

speaker: Ian
en: We could retell conclusions from published papers.

speaker: Erica
en: I'm not sure.

speaker: Erica
en: I mean, is that even allowed?

speaker: Erica
en: I know we don't have to use first-hand data we collect, but copying others?

speaker: Ian
en: Come on, it's not like we are using their findings as our own.

speaker: Ian
en: The professor said it would be okay as long as we cited our sources.

speaker: Erica
en: Oh, okay.

speaker: Ian
en: Do you have any ideas about stratovolcanoes?

speaker: Erica
en: Stratovolcanoes, also known as composite volcanoes, develop over many thousands of years through repeated eruptions and other volcanic activity.

speaker: Erica
en: But the current information I have at hand is only very vague.

speaker: Erica
en: Fuller descriptions are needed for this.

speaker: Ian
en: I'm sure you can find them.

speaker: Erica
en: Alright then.

speaker: Erica
en: Leave that to me.

speaker: Erica
en: Have you read anything about rhyolite caldera complexes?

speaker: Ian
en: Yeah, they are the most explosive volcanoes on Earth and don't even look like volcanoes.

speaker: Ian
en: The origin of these volcanoes, like Yellowstone, is associated with a hotspot.

speaker: Erica
en: You've got most of the information correct.

speaker: Erica
en: Unfortunately, the last bit you mentioned is wrong.

speaker: Erica
en: The origin of this type of volcano remains a mystery.

speaker: Erica
en: The hotspot origin theory doesn't work for most other rhyolite calderas.

speaker: Ian
en: Right.

speaker: Ian
en: I'll change that part.

speaker: Ian
en: What about the position on monogenetic fields?

speaker: Erica
en: It's shocking to see the photos of these immense volcanoes.

speaker: Erica
en: There are numerous monogenetic fields in the American southwest and Mexico.

speaker: Erica
en: But I got all the information from a website without specifying its source.

speaker: Erica
en: We'll have to examine the content again later.

speaker: Ian
en: Okay.

speaker: Ian
en: I can help with that.

speaker: Erica
en: Lastly, we have cinder cones.

speaker: Erica
en: What do you have on that?

speaker: Ian
en: Well, this type of volcano is usually tiny to the other volcanoes and thus sounds less attractive.

speaker: Ian
en: Maybe we should leave it out; it seems pretty unimportant to me.

speaker: Erica
en: I agree.

speaker: Erica
en: It's impossible to cover everything.

speaker: Ian
en: Erica, can you work on the delivery part?

speaker: Ian
en: I don't want to do it anymore.

speaker: Erica
en: I'm okay with that.

speaker: Erica
en: But why is that?

speaker: Ian
en: I was marked so low for the last presentation and didn't even have a clue why.

speaker: Ian
en: Maybe it's because I didn't include enough data on it.

speaker: Erica
en: Well, I'm not sure about that.

speaker: Erica
en: I think maybe you did not prepare the topic well enough.

speaker: Erica
en: You tried to explain the contributing factor forming the Ring of Fire in the Pacific Ocean, but you just read everything out loud without fully comprehending it yourself.

speaker: Ian
en: Right.

speaker: Ian
en: That must have been it.

speaker: Erica
en: We also have to look for supporting materials to illustrate the formation of different volcanoes.

speaker: Ian
en: What about documentaries?

speaker: Erica
en: They can be an excellent way to illustrate the volcanic eruption, but that might be too long for our presentation.

speaker: Erica
en: We've only got 20 minutes.

speaker: Ian
en: Have you considered adding photos to our handouts?

speaker: Erica
en: That's an option.

speaker: Erica
en: But people may focus on these photos rather than paying attention to what we have to say.

speaker: Erica
en: Maybe we can search online for part of a film about volcanoes and add it to the end of our presentation.

speaker: Ian
en: That would be better.

speaker: Ian
en: So how should we start our presentation?

speaker: Erica
en: Maybe we can first introduce some terms like extinct volcanoes and dormant volcanoes.

speaker: Ian
en: Why is that?

speaker: Erica
en: Even though they are widely-accepted terms among ordinary people, academia wouldn't accept them because there are no set definitions.

speaker: Erica
en: For example, an extinct volcano would be one nobody expects to erupt again.

speaker: Erica
en: But there have been several eruptions from widely-acknowledged 'extinct' volcanoes.

speaker: Ian
en: That's an excellent way to start our presentation.

speaker: Ian
en: What did the professor say about your last presentation?

speaker: Erica
en: There is still room for improvement.

speaker: Erica
en: She said that I had an in-depth analysis and provided enough statistics, but I didn't carry on and discuss my take on it.

speaker: Ian
en: Right.

speaker: Ian
en: So maybe we could also discuss the impact volcanoes have on humans and our planet.

speaker: Erica
en: Yeah.

speaker: Erica
en: There's little doubt that volcanoes have been instrumental in wiping out countless species during major extinction events.

speaker: Erica
en: But volcanic activity also shapes new habitats, enriching the soil with nutrients that allow life to thrive.

speaker: Erica
en: People haven't realised that there are abundant mineral resources waiting to be exploited in volcanoes.

speaker: Ian
en: I agree.

speaker: Ian
en: So now we can...
